title: 6 1 Eigenvalues and eigenvectors
body:
6.1 EIGENVALUES AND EIGENVECTORS

Given a square matrix A, a non-zero vector v is said to be an eigenvector of A if

Av = ╬╗v

for some ╬╗ Ōłł R. The number ╬╗ is referred to as the eigenvalue of A associated with v.

We first find eigenvalues by solving the characteristic equation det(A ŌłÆ ╬╗I) = 0. The eigenvectors are then found via row reduction and back substitution. A useful check to make sure you have all the eigenvalues is to use the fact that ╬Ż(eigenvalues) = Trace(A).

The zero vector is never an eigenvector but it is OK to have a zero eigenvalue.

If an n ├Ś n matrix A has n linearly independent eigenvectors and P is the matrix of eigenvectors (i.e., the eigenvectors form the columns of the matrix P ), then

P ŌłÆ1 AP = D

where D is the diagonal matrix of eigenvalues. The ordering of the eigenvalues in D must match the ordering of the eigenvectors in P . This process is referred to as diagonalisation.

Square matrices transform vectors, stretching them and rotating them in space. As an example, consider what happens when the following matrix multiplies a vector:      1 4 1 = ŌłÆ3 9 0

But now consider      1 4 2 = ŌłÆ3 9 3

Observe that  A  simply makes this vector 7 times as long ŌĆö there is no rotation. We say that v = is an eigenvector of A with associated eigenvalue ╬╗ = 7.

More generally, an eigenvector v is a special vector that, when multiplied by A, simply gives back v multiplied by some number ╬╗,

Av = ╬╗v, (v 6= 0).

The eigenvalue ╬╗ can be greater than one (the vector v is lengthened), less than one (the vector v is shortened), negative (the direction of v is reversed) or even zero (the vector v has zero length). However, the vector v must be non-zero to be an eigenvector.

To find the eigenvectors and eigenvalues of a matrix A we rewrite the above equation as

Av ŌłÆ ╬╗v = Av ŌłÆ ╬╗Iv = (A ŌłÆ ╬╗I)v = 0.

Since v 6= 0 then the matrix (A ŌłÆ ╬╗I) must be non-invertible. This means that

det(A ŌłÆ ╬╗I) = 0.

This is called the characteristic equation and it is a polynomial equation for the eigenvalue ╬╗. A 2 ├Ś 2 matrix will have a quadratic characteristic equation and a 3 ├Ś 3 matrix will have a cubic characteristic equation. Once you have the eigenvalues you can then find the eigenvectors by solving (A ŌłÆ ╬╗I)v = 0 using row reduction.   1 4 Example 1 Find all the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of A = and hence ŌłÆ3 9 find an invertible matrix P and a diagonal matrix D such that P ŌłÆ1 AP = D.

    2 2 3 0 F P = , D= F 1 3 0 7

’Ż½ ’ŻČ ŌłÆ2 2 ŌłÆ3 Example 2 Find all the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of A = ’ŻŁ 2 1 ŌłÆ6 ’ŻĖ and ŌłÆ1 ŌłÆ2 0 hence diagonalise A.

We begin by finding the eigenvalues of A. In this case the matrix is 3 ├Ś 3 so the characteristic polynomial will be a cubic equation. These are difficult to solve so we need to work carefully through the determinant to see if we can find a common factor.

The eigenvalues are ╬╗ = ŌłÆ3, ŌłÆ3, 5. As a check we use the fact that the sum of eigenvectors must be the same as the trace of A (the sum of the diagonal components of A):

ŌłÆ3 ŌłÆ 3 + 5 = ŌłÆ2 + 1 + 0 = ŌłÆ1. Note that we have a double eigenvalue ╬╗ = ŌłÆ3. However, this does not mean that we will be short one eigenvector. In fact, there will be two eigenvectors associated with the eigenvalue ╬╗ = ŌłÆ3.

LetŌĆÖs now find the eigenvectors:

Lastly we form the matrix of eigenvectors P and the matrix of eigenvalues D, being careful to make sure that the order of the eigenvectors matches the order of the eigenvalues.

’Ż½ ’ŻČ ’Ż½ ’ŻČ ŌłÆ1 ŌłÆ2 3 5 0 0 F P = ’ŻŁ ŌłÆ2 1 0 ’ŻĖ , D = ’ŻŁ 0 ŌłÆ3 0 ’ŻĖ F 1 0 1 0 0 ŌłÆ3

What is happening with the process of diagonalisation? Remember that a square matrix A will transform a vector, but it can only stretch or shrink its eigenvector by a factor ╬╗. So if we put the eigenvectors in the columns of a matrix P and then multiply by the matrix A, the result will be that each column will simply be multiplied by its corresponding eigenvalue ’Ż▒ ’Ż½ ’ŻČ ’Ż½ ’ŻČ ’Ż½ ’ŻČ’Ż╝ ’Ż▓ ŌłÆ1 ŌłÆ2 3 ’ŻĮ AP = P D = 5 ├Ś ’ŻŁ ŌłÆ2 ’ŻĖ , ŌłÆ3 ├Ś ’ŻŁ 1 ’ŻĖ , ŌłÆ3 ├Ś ’ŻŁ 0 ’ŻĖ . 1 0 1 ’Ż│ ’ŻŠ

We can then multiply on the right by P ŌłÆ1 on each side of the equation to get

P ŌłÆ1 AP = D.

What this does is transform the matrix A to a special basis where it takes on its simplest form, stretching or shrinking its eigenvectors in each direction.

Although the diagonal matrix D is the simplest form of A, it is not unique. If we rearrange the columns of the matrix P so that the eigenvectors are in a different order, the corresponding eigenvectors in D will also be re-ordered. The order of eigenvalues in D always has to match the order of the eigenvectors in P . The matrix P is also not unique, because each eigenvector is only defined up to a multiplicative constant.

You can now do Q 90
